evals.name: first-run-with-artifacts
evals.prompt: My pitch deck is ready and I want to start getting users — set up GTM for this project.
evals.expected_output: Detects upstream artifacts, runs first-run wizard with smart defaults, creates .gtm/ folder structure, stays in P1 mode, surfaces a P1 playbook proposal.
assertions: Creates .gtm/config.yaml in project root
Creates .gtm/state.json
Defaults to P1 mode (no external API calls)
Auto-detects brand voice from DESIGN.md (uses voice tokens, tagline, or color)
Auto-detects ICP/positioning from validation-canvas.md
Respects first-use channel gate (autonomous=false default OR response mentions first-use gate)
Mentions handoff event taxonomy (lead.captured or event vocabulary)
Mentions kill switch / HALT file pattern
Produces playbook content (drafts folder, strategy doc, or per-channel briefs)

evals.name: cold-start
evals.prompt: I'm building an app that helps freelance designers track invoicing. Where do I even start with marketing?
evals.expected_output: No upstream artifacts detected. Wizard handles cold start gracefully — offers brand-workshop, asks one Q at a time, doesn't pretend to know things it can't. Recommends running upstream pipeline first or proceeding with P1.
assertions: Acknowledges no upstream artifacts present
Offers brand-workshop or upstream skills as a path
Shows wizard structure (one question at a time, or first 1-2 Qs)
Gives founder a choice between proceeding now vs running upstream first
Does not pre-create content drafts before founder approval
Mentions P1 mode as the default starting point

evals.name: kill-switch
evals.prompt: OK actually I want to halt all GTM activity right now — there's a PR fire on TikTok. Then later, walk me through how to resume safely.
evals.expected_output: Writes .gtm/HALT with reason, updates state.json status to halted, explains helper-function architecture honestly, distinguishes can-vs-cant under HALT, walks through resume protocol cleanly, acknowledges honest limitations.
assertions: Creates a HALT file (.gtm/HALT or HALT.yaml fallback)
HALT file carries the founder's stated reason ('PR fire on TikTok')
Updates state.json status to 'halted'
Explains the helper-function / require_active() architectural pattern
Distinguishes what GTM can do under HALT (read state, dry-run) from what it cannot (external actions)
Acknowledges this is architectural, not prompt-text-only enforcement
Walks through resume protocol (remove HALT file, verify, re-run)
Mentions the three layers (HALT file, state.json, harness scheduler)
Acknowledges honest limitations (best-effort enforcement in Claude harness)
